Refer to table 2. Based on the new net value, how much is the opportunity cost of the best choice? 

Group of answer choices

Opportunity costs= $35 net value lost from 3 meals

Opportunity costs=$39 net value lost from 3 CDs

Opportunity costs=$33 net value lost from jeans 

Opportunity costs= $33 net value lost from 3 meals
Opportunity costs=$33 net value lost from 3 meals

After purchasing a cup of coffee from your local gas station for $2.25, you can always refill your cup for only $0.75 each time. Suppose one day you get four refills. The marginal cost of the 4th refill at the gas station is:
Group of answer choices

$0.25

$3

$0.5

$0.75
The marginal cost of the 4th refill at the gas station is $0.75.

Suppose the gym charges a $5 entrance fee for each visit. Table 3 provides hypothetical data on the values Dave derives from each gym visit. For example, Dave gets $20 from the 1st visit, $15 from the 2nd, $7 from the 3rd, and so on. According to marginal principles, how many visits should David take to the gym each week? 

Table 3

 Weekly visits

Marginal Benefit (in $)

  1st visit  

20

 2nd visit

15

3rd visit

10

4th visit

4

5th visit

3

 6th visit

 0



Group of answer choices

1 visit.

2 visits.

3 visits.

4 visits.

5 visits..

6 visits..

0 visit.
According to marginal principles, David should take 3 visits to the gym each week.

Suppose the price of the gym membership per visit increases to $10. Refer to table 3. According to marginal principles, how many visits each week should David take to the gym at the new price?

According to marginal principles, David should take 2 visits to the gym each week at the new price.

If David receives a coupon allowing him to take each visit for $3 for a month. Refer to table 3. According to marginal principles, how many visits each week should David take to the gym at the discounted price?

Suppose your monthly non-refundable gym membership fee is $20. You get $5 worth of marginal benefit from each workout session. How much is your sunk cost?
Group of answer choices

0

$10

$20

$110
Your sunk cost in this scenario is $20.

You decide to drive a rental car on a long road trip of 1,500 miles. The car allows you drive 1,000 miles for non-refundable $29.99/day rental. Any additional mile thereafter would cost $0.1. If you drive 1,500 miles, how much is your marginal cost?
Group of answer choices

$0.1

$0.5

$29.99

$50
Your marginal cost for driving 1,500 miles would be $50.

You decide to drive a rental car on a long road trip of 1,500 miles. The car allows you drive 1,000 miles for non-refundable $29.99/day rental. Any additional mile thereafter would cost $0.1. If you drive 1,500 miles, how much is your sunk cost?
Group of answer choices

$0.1

$0.5

$29.99

$50
Your sunk cost in this scenario is $29.99, as it is a non-refundable rental fee for driving up to 1,000 miles.
